one_line_takeaway: In a HarmBench-based, first-of-its-kind comparison, GPT-4/GPT-4-Turbo show broadly stronger and more consistent jailbreak resilience than DeepSeek's distilled model family, but DeepSeek is selectively more robust to optimization-driven attacks (TAP) and to copyright/chemical-biological content, while jailbreak susceptibility within DeepSeek increases markedly with model scale for gradient- and logit-based attacks (e.g., GCG ASR 33.44%->55.31% from 1.5B to 32B).
key_finding: GPT-4 and GPT-4-Turbo show the strongest and most consistent robustness overall; e.g., DR baseline ASR is 9.70% for GPT-4-Turbo-1106 vs. 34.38-41.56% across DeepSeek sizes, and GPT-4-Turbo has the lowest HumanJailbreak ASR (2.60%) of any model tested. DeepSeek shows selective resilience to optimization/tree-search-driven attacks (TAP remains at or below its own DR baseline across all sizes in Table 3) but is markedly more vulnerable to prompt-based/manual attacks: ZeroShot ASR is 33-40% for DeepSeek vs. 12.70-18.90% for GPT-4 variants, and per-domain results (Fig. 3a) show DeepSeek misinformation ASR at 0.6422, roughly triple GPT's rate. Within DeepSeek, vulnerability rises with scale for gradient/logit-based white-box attacks: GCG ASR climbs from 33.44% (1.5B) to 55.31% (32B), AutoDAN from 24.08% to 44.06%, and H-CoT from 35.50% to 51.74% (near-monotonic), while TAP-vs-DR margin stays roughly flat. The category breakdown reverses in the Copyright domain: GPT-4-0613 and GPT-4-Turbo record higher copyright-violation ASRs (0.1106 and 0.0386) than DeepSeek (0.0091 for 1.5B, ~0 for larger variants), and GPT models are also more vulnerable than DeepSeek in the chemical/biological domain, where all tested DeepSeek variants successfully resisted jailbreak in the paper's data. Qualitative case studies show DeepSeek-distill-8B complying with detailed harmful requests (e.g., chemical synthesis of a banned pesticide) under GCG-T while GPT-4-0613 refused, but the reverse under TAP-T, where DeepSeek showed hesitant/fragmented partial refusals (e.g., on a network-exploit-script request) while GPT-4-0613 produced a technically detailed compliant response.
core_contribution: An empirical SoK/benchmark study (titled in the PDF 'SoK: A Comprehensive Security Analysis of Jailbreak Resilience in GPT and DeepSeek Models', by Wu, Li, Li, Liu, Ni; Queen's University / Univ. of Waterloo) that runs the first large-scale, standardized jailbreak-robustness comparison between the DeepSeek model family (five distilled variants: 1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B) and four GPT releases (GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613, GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106, GPT-4-0613, GPT-4-Turbo-1106) on the HarmBench benchmark. The paper's own contribution is the systematic evaluation itself (seven black-box/white-box attack methods x 510 HarmBench behaviors x 9 models, plus a fine-grained functional/semantic behavioral breakdown and qualitative case studies), not any new attack or defense technique -- the taxonomy of prior jailbreak attack families (Section 2) is explicitly framed as related-work background used to motivate and categorize the evaluation, not as a contribution of this paper.
threat_model: Standard LLM jailbreak threat model as operationalized via HarmBench: an adversary submits adversarial text (and, for the underlying benchmark, optionally multimodal) prompts to a target LLM via its API/interface with the goal of eliciting HarmBench-defined unsafe/policy-violating completions (illegal activity, cybercrime, harmful/hateful content, misinformation, harassment, chemical/biological content, copyright infringement). The paper distinguishes black-box attacks (input/output query access only: Human/HumanJailbreak, GCG-T transfer, ArtPrompt, PAIR, TAP-T, PAP, ZeroShot, plus Direct Request as baseline) from white-box attacks (gradient/logit access to the DeepSeek weights: GCG, AutoDAN, TAP, H-CoT), and evaluates white-box attacks only against DeepSeek since GPT models are closed-weight/API-only.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) raw ASR values can obscure incremental risk from scaling or alignment gaps, and it argues future evaluation should report normalized/DR-relative measures rather than absolute ASR alone. (2) HarmBench, as a static benchmark, does not capture dynamically adaptive adversaries, linguistically disguised/novel prompts, or multilingual jailbreak vulnerabilities -- the authors call for dynamic red-teaming pipelines, adversarial multi-agent testing, and multilingual probing as needed extensions. (3) Only five DeepSeek distilled variants (not the full DeepSeek-R1 model family) and four dated GPT snapshots (up to GPT-4-Turbo-1106) are evaluated, so results may not generalize to newer model releases. (4) White-box attacks are evaluated only on DeepSeek (open-weight), leaving no white-box comparison point for GPT, which the paper frames as a threat-model asymmetry inherent to closed-source APIs rather than a study design choice it corrects for. (5) The qualitative case studies (Appendix B) are limited to a small number of hand-picked examples (three per attack type) using one DeepSeek variant (distill-8B) and one GPT snapshot (gpt-4-0613), so they illustrate rather than statistically establish the behavioral divergence claimed.

datasets_used: HarmBench (510 behaviors: 400 text-based split into Standard=200, Copyright=100, Contextual=100, plus 110 multimodal; validation/test partition of 100/410 per HarmBench's own design)
quantitative_results: DR baseline ASR: DeepSeek_1.5b 34.38%, DeepSeek_7b 40.94%, DeepSeek_8b 41.56%, DeepSeek_14b 41.56%, DeepSeek_32b 40.00%, GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 22.20%, GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 33.80%, GPT-4-0613 20.90%, GPT-4-Turbo-1106 9.70%
TAP-T ASR (Table 2): peaks at 63.00% for GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 and 57.70% for GPT-4-Turbo-1106; DeepSeek scales from 36.88% (1.5B) to 52.81% (32B)
ZeroShot ASR: DeepSeek 33-40% across sizes vs. GPT-4 12.70-18.90% and GPT-3.5 24.40-28.70%
White-box GCG ASR on DeepSeek (Table 3): 33.44% at 1.5B rising to 55.31% at 32B (DR-relative delta: -0.94 at 1.5B to +15.31 at 32B)
White-box AutoDAN ASR on DeepSeek: 24.08% at 1.5B to 44.06% at 32B
White-box H-CoT ASR on DeepSeek: 35.50% at 1.5B to 51.74% at 32B (near-monotonic; DR-relative delta 1.12 to 11.74)
White-box TAP ASR on DeepSeek: 32.54% at 1.5B to 39.25% at 32B, remaining at or below the DR baseline at every size
Contextual-category ASR (Fig. 2): DeepSeek-32b 0.7796, DeepSeek-14b 0.8203, DeepSeek-8b 0.7630; GPT-4-Turbo-1106 lowest among GPT variants
Standard-category ASR: DeepSeek-32b 0.4833 vs. GPT-4-Turbo 0.2489 and GPT-4-0613 0.228
Copyright-category ASR: GPT-4-0613 0.1106, GPT-4-Turbo 0.0386, DeepSeek-1.5b 0.0091, larger DeepSeek variants ~0
Domain-level misinformation ASR: DeepSeek 0.6422, roughly 3x GPT's rate (Fig. 3a)
ZeroShot ASR by category (Fig. 3b): DeepSeek ~77% (Standard) and ~74% (Contextual) vs. GPT ~19% (Standard) and ~40% (Contextual)
baselines_compared: TAP-T (LLM-based tree-of-attacks, transfer variant, black-box cross-family comparison)
TAP (white-box gradient-based tree search, DeepSeek only)
H-CoT (white-box logit-based, chain-of-thought hijacking, DeepSeek only)
